region_of_origin: Taiwan (popularised across East and Southeast Asia, including Singapore)
food_subgroup: Sweetened milk tea with tapioca pearls (bubble tea)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_03311737 covers bubble tea as a category. No exact code exists for brown sugar boba milk tea specifically; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKW covers tea-based beverages broadly. No specific code for brown sugar boba milk tea; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for brown sugar boba milk tea or bubble tea as a food product.

preparation_method: tapioca pearls cooked and coated in brown sugar syrup
served chilled or at room temperature

serving.description: 1 cup (approximately 500 ml / 500 g including pearls and liquid)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one 500 ml cup containing brewed tea, fresh dairy milk, brown sugar syrup, and cooked tapioca pearls, as typically served by specialty bubble tea chains in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Bubble tea (milk tea with tapioca pearls) — nutrient composition estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: whole milk (FDC ID 1097512), brewed black tea (FDC ID 1104262), tapioca pearls cooked (FDC ID 168875), and brown sugar (FDC ID 168833); proportions based on standard 500 ml brown sugar boba milk tea formulation (approximately 40% milk, 30% brewed tea, 15% cooked tapioca pearls, 15% brown sugar syrup by weight)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for brown sugar boba milk tea. Values estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central entries for whole milk, brewed black tea, cooked tapioca pearls, and brown sugar in proportions representative of a standard 500 ml serving. Per-100g values derived from the blended composition. This is a medium-confidence estimate; values should be verified against laboratory analysis or a branded nutrition panel when available.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of tapioca-based foods and sweetened milk beverages — analogous estimate based on: (1) cooked tapioca pearls GI ~70 (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283); (2) whole milk GI ~27 (University of Sydney GI Database); (3) brown sugar GI ~65 (University of Sydney GI Database); weighted blend for standard brown sugar boba milk tea formulation yields estimated GI ~62
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for brown sugar boba milk tea. GI estimated as a weighted average of component GI values: cooked tapioca pearls (~70, Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care), whole milk (~27, University of Sydney GI Database), and brown sugar (~65, University of Sydney GI Database), weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution in a standard 500 ml serving. Estimated GI ~62 (medium). Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (62 × 92.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 57.4, rounded to 57.2. This is an estimate; direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content and high sugar load contributing to overall dietary quality concerns
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 ml serving contributes approximately 140 mg sodium and a high sugar load (~65 g total sugars); individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to occasional treats and prefer unsweetened or reduced-sugar variants to support blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from brown sugar syrup and cooked tapioca pearls causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: One 500 ml serving delivers an estimated glycemic load of approximately 57, driven by rapidly digestible tapioca starch and free sugars from brown sugar syrup; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this beverage and monitor postprandial glucose if consumed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content and saturated fat from dairy milk contributing to atherogenic dietary pattern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high free sugar content (~65 g per serving) and saturated fat from whole dairy milk may adversely affect lipid profiles and cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this beverage as an occasional indulgence and prefer lower-sugar, lower-fat alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Brown Sugar Boba Milk Tea (黑糖波波奶茶) is a popular sweetened milk tea beverage originating from Taiwan and widely consumed across East and Southeast Asia, including Singapore. It is prepared by brewing black tea, combining it with fresh whole dairy milk, and adding a generous quantity of brown sugar syrup that is often caramelised to create a distinctive tiger-stripe pattern on the cup wall. Cooked tapioca pearls (boba or bobo), which are chewy, starchy spheres made from tapioca starch, are added to the drink and are typically pre-coated or simmered in brown sugar syrup to enhance their sweetness and colour. The resulting beverage is rich, creamy, and intensely sweet, with a caramel-like depth from the brown sugar and a satisfying chewy texture from the tapioca pearls. In Singapore, premium versions such as the Heytea Supreme Brown Sugar Bobo Milk Tea use real dairy milk rather than non-dairy creamer, distinguishing them from lower-cost variants. Per 100 g, the drink provides approximately 95 kcal, 1.8 g protein, 1.6 g fat, and 18.5 g carbohydrate, with 13 g sugars. A standard 500 ml serving delivers an estimated glycemic load of approximately 57, making it a high-glycemic-load beverage unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or obesity. The beverage contains dairy and is not suitable for lactose-intolerant individuals or vegans. It is generally considered halal and gluten-free. Clinically, the primary concerns are the very high free sugar content, the high glycemic load from tapioca starch and brown sugar, and the caloric density of a single serving (~475 kcal per 500 ml cup).

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central branded, or equivalent) provides a direct nutrient panel for brown sugar boba milk tea as a finished product. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods and SR Legacy entries for whole milk, brewed black tea, cooked tapioca pearls, and brown sugar in proportions representative of a standard 500 ml serving. GI is estimated from component GI values using University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Medium confidence is appropriate given the estimation methodology.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (whole milk FDC 1097512, brewed black tea FDC 1104262, cooked tapioca FDC 168875, brown sugar FDC 168833) blended in proportions for a standard 500 ml brown sugar boba milk tea. Per-100g values are derived from this blend. Direct laboratory analysis or a branded nutrition panel from Heytea would improve accuracy. 2. GI is an analogous estimate weighted from component GI values; direct measurement is recommended. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero by formulation as this product contains no partially hydrogenated oils; dairy-derived trans fats are negligible and reported as 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy whole milk data. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.4) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1) are estimated from USDA whole milk fatty acid profile scaled to the blended formulation. 5. Sentinel values (-1) are not used in this record; all micronutrients are estimated from USDA component data. 6. The food_name has been normalised to the canonical form 'Brown Sugar Boba Milk Tea'; the input name 'Supreme Brown Sugar Bobo Milk Tea' is preserved as an alias. 7. Halal status is assumed based on ingredients (dairy milk, tea, tapioca, brown sugar) with no pork or alcohol; formal halal certification status of Heytea Singapore should be verified independently. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (62 × 92.5) / 100 = 57.35, reported as 57.2 (minor rounding). Available carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 18.5 g/100g × 500 g / 100 = 92.5 g.
